stem: By the Fundamental Theorem of Finite Abelian Groups, every finite abelian group is (isomorphic to) a direct product of cyclic groups. What is the order of the abelian group \( G = \mathbb{Z}_{[[n1]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n2]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n3]]} \)?
solution: The order of a direct product of groups is the product of their orders: \( |G| = [[n1]]\cdot[[n2]]\cdot[[n3]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the factor sizes instead of multiplying them.
This computes the LCM of the factor sizes, which gives the maximum element order, not the group's order.
This omits the third factor \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n3]]} \) entirely.
This omits the first factor \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n1]]} \) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct order.
This is off by one from the correct order.

stem: Consider the element \( ([[a]],[[b]],[[c]]) \) of the direct product \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[k]]} \). Its order is the least common multiple of the orders of [[a]] in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \), [[b]] in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), and [[c]] in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[k]]} \). What is the order of \( ([[a]],[[b]],[[c]]) \)?
solution: The order of [[a]] in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \) is \( [[m]]/\gcd([[m]],[[a]]) \), of [[b]] in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) is \( [[n]]/\gcd([[n]],[[b]]) \), and of [[c]] in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[k]]} \) is \( [[k]]/\gcd([[k]],[[c]]) \). The order of the triple is the LCM of these three numbers, which is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the three component orders instead of taking their LCM.
This adds the three component orders instead of taking their LCM.
This is the order of the WHOLE group, not of this one element.
This drops the third component's order from the LCM entirely.
This is off by one from the correct order.
This is off by one from the correct order.

stem: Let \( G = \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \). How many elements \( g \in G \) satisfy \( [[d]]\cdot g = (0,0) \) (equivalently, whose order divides [[d]])?
solution: In \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \), the elements \( x \) with \( [[d]]x=0 \) form a subgroup of size \( \gcd([[m]],[[d]]) \); likewise \( \gcd([[n]],[[d]]) \) in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \). Since \( G \) is a direct product, the count of such pairs is the PRODUCT \( \gcd([[m]],[[d]])\cdot\gcd([[n]],[[d]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two component counts instead of multiplying them (the pairs combine independently, by the multiplication principle).
This counts the ENTIRE group \( G \), ignoring the order-[[d]] restriction.
This uses the LCM of each modulus with [[d]] instead of the gcd, which is unrelated to the solution count.
This only relates the two moduli to each other, ignoring [[d]] entirely.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.
